oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Citation Analysis for Acquisitions Reveals Better Grades for Students Using Library Resources
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Nunnelly-Stewart, Amanda
Horne, Brandy
Warren, Amanda
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This study investigates the use of library resources by first-year students (English 101/102) at the University of South Carolina Aiken (USCA) with the goal of identifying resources students use outside of the library. The study was originally designed for acquisition purposes; however, the data revealed a strong connection between library resource use and assignment grades. While the citation analysis revealed that students with higher grades used more academic resources, it is unclear if using these resources led to better grades or if stronger students were simply more likely to utilize those resources. This data can be used to show that library resources including instruction have an impact on student success and can be reflected in assignment grades. Libraries can also use this data to demonstrate library value because it shows that students who apply what they learned in library instructional sessions find greater success on research projects. Keywordscitation analysis, library instruction, student success, grades, source evaluation
oai_dc:dc.dc:publisher: Association for Information Science and Technology
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 Amanda R. Nunnelly-Stewart, Brandy R. Horne, Dr. Amanda R. Warren
